scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aeschines is a 4th-century Athenian orator discussing the 'euthyna' (audit) process required of all Athenian magistrates.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The speech 'Against Ctesiphon' was delivered in 330 BCE, addressing laws regarding the crowning of officials.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule concerns the location of honorary proclamations in the Athenian Assembly (Pnyx) as dictated by Athenian law.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The speech 'Against Ctesiphon' was delivered in 330 BCE, focusing on administrative and ceremonial regulations.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aeschines describes 'isegoria' (equal right of speech), a defining characteristic of the Athenian democratic constitution.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Associated with Aeschines' floruit and the delivery of this speech in 330 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The 'Three Hundred' and 'trierarchs' are specific fiscal and military institutions of the Athenian state.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Refers to the trierarchic reforms of Demosthenes around 340 BCE, which Aeschines critiques in his 330 BCE speech.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Disfranchisement (atimia) for bribery was a standard penalty in the Athenian judicial system.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Stated by Aeschines in 330 BCE as a standing legal norm in the Athenian courts.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The Council of the Areopagus and the prosecution for cowardice/treason are specific to Athenian law and the aftermath of Chaeronea.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Refers to the emergency measures and prosecutions following the Battle of Chaeronea in 338 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Andocides is an Athenian orator discussing the shared rights and benefits of the Athenian citizen body.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Andocides' speech 'On His Return' is dated to approximately 407 BCE during his attempt to regain civic rights.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule describes the Panhellenic but locally enforced norm of 'hikesia' (supplication) at the sacred hearth of the Athenian Council.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Andocides refers to his actions during the oligarchy of the Four Hundred in 411 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The Council of Five Hundred (Boule) and its accountability to the Demos are central pillars of Athenian democracy.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Presented as a contemporary constitutional fact in Andocides' speech around 407 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The granting of citizenship or 'proxenia' to foreigners and slaves for 'euergetism' (benefaction) was a common Athenian practice.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Andocides cites this as a current practice of the Athenian people in 407 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The power of the Athenian Demos to grant 'adeia' (immunity/pardon) was a sovereign right of the citizen assembly.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Refers to the pardons and subsequent revocations related to the Herms affair in 415 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The 'Four Hundred' was a specific oligarchic coup in Athens that briefly overthrew the democracy.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Refers specifically to the oligarchic revolution of the Four Hundred in 411 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The Heliastic Oath and the procedural protections for citizens are core components of Athenian law.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The speech 'Against Alcibiades' (though of debated authenticity) is set around the ostracism of 416 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Ostracism was a uniquely Athenian institution used to exile prominent citizens without a trial.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The verbatim refers to the last historical instance of ostracism in Athens, occurring in 416 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: In Athens, women could theoretically initiate divorce by filing a 'apoleipsis' with the Archon Eponymos.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Refers to the historical attempt by Hipparete to divorce Alcibiades, roughly around 424 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule concerns 'symbola' (judicial treaties) between Athens and other Greek city-states regarding the rights of free men.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The speech is set during the mid-Peloponnesian War era, approximately 416 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: This refers to the 'epobelia' or similar fines in the Athenian judicial system to discourage frivolous lawsuits ('sykophancy').
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Contextually dated to the era of the speech's setting in the late 5th century BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The principle of 'bis in idem' (double jeopardy) was codified in the Athenian legal code.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This legal principle was a cornerstone of the restored democracy's laws after 403 BCE, though cited here in a 5th-century context.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The exclusion of 'metoikoi' (resident aliens) from performing in certain prestigious civic choruses was a matter of Athenian law.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Refers to the rules of the Dionysia or Lenaia festivals in the 5th century BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Describes the treatment of prisoners of war according to the decree passed by the Athenian Assembly concerning Melos.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The destruction of Melos and the enslavement of its population occurred in 416 BCE.
